findings: The hot-pink section headline reads as incomplete in the screenshot: “Make the scroll count in” lands without the payoff visible, which makes the section feel broken rather than bold.
The primary CTA in the hero is visually too small and too lightweight compared with the oversized headline, so the page sells attitude harder than action.
The tiny cyan plus and pink chevron in the hero feel stray and disconnected from the layout, reading like leftover decoration instead of intentional brand assets.
The long supporting copy on black uses a dim gray that drops contrast and gets hard to read at this size, especially in the hero and proof sections.
The three black cards in the white section are visually identical, so the eye gets no clear entry point or priority across the row.

The page relies on large black-white-pink horizontal bands with very little playful TikTok-specific visual character, which makes the system feel more generic campaign landing page than creator-native.
Several headlines are so wide and heavy that line breaks feel awkward and crowded, especially the hero and the “stories.” section, weakening hierarchy instead of sharpening it.

events.prompt: Audit the landing page against our brand rules, focusing on adherence to our color palette, font usage, and overall tone. Ensure the page is free of business jargon and maintains a playful, direct sty

events.doing: auditing the page against brand rules

findings: The hero headline uses two emphasis colors at once: a cyan plus mark and pink word/chevron, which breaks the one-accent emphasis rule and makes the headline feel less disciplined.

In the pink 'How it works' section, the emphasized final word in the headline is effectively invisible against the same pink background, so the headline reads as incomplete ('Make less fuss. Get more').
The large dot pattern on the white section is rendered in light grey, introducing a non-brand neutral that reads off-brand and filler-like.
The hero supporting copy runs too wide in a single long line, weakening scanability and making the hierarchy feel loose under such a bold headline.
The oversized grey dot block takes up too much visual weight relative to the actual message in the 'The problem' section, so the decorative element competes with the content instead of supporting it.

The three black cards in the white section feel dense and text-heavy, with small body copy sitting in large dark boxes, which hurts legibility and makes the section feel more like placeholder content than polished brand storytelling.
The hero leaves a large empty black void on the right, making the opening feel unbalanced and unfinished instead of bold and intentional.
The small cyan plus and pink chevron in the hero read like stray marks rather than purposeful brand elements because they float with too little visual weight.
The hot-pink 'How it works' headline appears cut off as shown, reading only 'Make the scroll count in' and feeling incomplete.
The 'Proof' section uses vague stat cards like '60s', '1 feed', and 'More taps' that look like placeholder claims instead of concrete evidence.
The page relies on the same black rounded card block three sections in a row, which flattens hierarchy and makes the layout feel repetitive.
Body copy inside the black cards is noticeably small and dense relative to the oversized headlines, which weakens scanning and legibility.
The pink emphasis treatment is over-relied on across section headlines, so the highlighted words stop feeling special and the hierarchy loses punch.
The CTA row in the hero feels visually undersized compared with the headline block, so the primary action does not carry enough weight.
The hero headline mixes cyan and pink emphasis at once: the cyan plus mark competes with the pink word and pink chevron, breaking the one-accent focus.
The large pale grey dot pattern in the white section introduces a neutral visual field that feels off-palette instead of using only black, white, and approved brand colors.
The dot pattern on the right is visibly cropped off at the edge, which makes the section look unfinished and template-like.
The problem headline breaks awkwardly so the emphasized pink word 'TikToks.' sits as an isolated fragment in the middle of the sentence, weakening readability and hierarchy.
There is a large block of empty black space under the hero CTAs before the next section starts, making the opening composition feel sparse and underdesigned.
The repeated black cards in both the white section and pink section are visually monotonous, so the page starts to feel rigid instead of playful or creatively paced.

events.doing: applying this turn's critique findings
revising the page from recalled traces

findings: The hero leaves a large empty black void to the right and below the CTAs, which makes the first screen feel unfinished instead of energetic or content-rich.
The tiny cyan plus and tiny pink chevron in the hero read like stray punctuation marks, not confident brand devices, so they add clutter without helping hierarchy.
The hero subhead and supporting paragraph are too similar in size and weight, so the copy hierarchy under the main headline feels mushy.
The body copy inside the black cards is small and dense relative to the oversized headings, which hurts fast scan legibility.
The hot-pink 'How it works' band loses a clear focal point because the headline has no visible emphasis word, so that section reads flatter than the others.
The 'Proof' section uses vague stat labels like '1 feed' and 'More taps' that look like filler rather than credible proof, weakening results-oriented trust.
The section chips ('Why it works', 'How it works', 'Proof') are visually too small and timid compared with the headline scale, so section starts are easy to miss.
The page relies heavily on repeated black rectangles with similar sizing and treatment, which makes the middle sections feel monotonous instead of playful or creatively varied.

events.doing: applying this turn's critique findings
revising the page from recalled traces

events.reasoning: The page has strong contrast, a clear hero hierarchy, and mostly sharp, persuasive copy, but the later sections feel less resolved: the white section is airy to the point of sparse, and the pink three-step section looks awkwardly cropped and imbalanced with the oversized black shape colliding with the cards. It reads broadly TikTok-like through the black/white/#FE2C55 palette and direct tone, but brand fit is incomplete because the typography appears generic rather than distinctly Sofia Pro, the cyan accent is essentially absent, and the decorative pale outline/extra shape language weakens the tightly controlled brand system. quality 78, brandAdherence 62 → score 72 (0.6·q + 0.4·b).

events.flaw: The hero headline mixes cyan and pink emphasis at once: the cyan plus mark competes with the pink word and pink chevron, breaking the one-accent focus.
events.brandRuleCited: no more than one emphasis color per headline

events.flaw: The large pale grey dot pattern in the white section introduces a neutral visual field that feels off-palette instead of using only black, white, and approved brand colors.
The dot pattern on the right is visibly cropped off at the edge, which makes the section look unfinished and template-like.
The problem headline breaks awkwardly so the emphasized pink word 'TikToks.' sits as an isolated fragment in the middle of the sentence, weakening readability and hierarchy.
There is a large block of empty black space under the hero CTAs before the next section starts, making the opening composition feel sparse and underdesigned.
events.brandRuleCited: Forget to have some fun along the way

events.flaw: The repeated black cards in both the white section and pink section are visually monotonous, so the page starts to feel rigid instead of playful or creatively paced.
